posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: I came very close to pouring this $70 bottle of Chardonnay down the drain. First bottle ever from this producer and it will certainly be my last. It was the palest imaginable yellow in color and simply reeked of wood. I don’t know where this winemaker falls in the pecking order of Burg producers, but I can’t conceive of anyone enjoying this wine. Nasty, nasty stuff!

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Sorry about your luck. Sounds like it could probably could use some bottle age. They’re not all bad, but definitely the “lesser” Leflaive and I think overpriced trying to capitalize on the name. Not a producer I seek out. I did enjoy the 2017 Batard earlier this year, but there’s plenty of other GC White Burg I’d purchase first for $450. I also had a 2010 CC that was premox.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I’ve had a dozen or so assorted bottles from “the other Leflaive” over the years, usually the village Puligny or Chassagne, and they’ve always been pretty good. They usually get decent marks on WB too.

Maybe just too young + a weaker vintage? I don’t know.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 2018 is maybe not the vintage to dip toes in communal appellation négociant wines, given the yields, but I wouldn’t write off the producer wholesale: anything labelled “récolte du Domaine”, especially the ex-Domaine Leflaive holdings, has been excellent for a while; to the extent that, rather than feeling that they’re capitalizing on the name, the results of a blind tasting might be in doubt.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I have not had any 2018s from Olivier Leflaive but tasted some 2017s at the Paulee Grand Tasting in 2020. I had not had any of their wines in many years and liked the wines I had more than I expected.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
I have not had any 2018s from Olivier Leflaive but tasted some 2017s at the Paulee Grand Tasting in 2020. I had not had any of their wines in many years and liked the wines I had more than I expected.

O Leflaive wines used to suck. Haven’t had recent versions.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
O Leflaive wines used to suck.
[/quote]

Which is why I was surprised when I liked the 2017s I tasted.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: Interesting (at least to me) epilogue with this wine. I drank this over 2 days. Because of my lack of enthusiasm, the final pour sat in the glass for a considerable time before I approached it. The wine had warmed significantly and much to my surprise most of the oak had blown off and it had put on considerable weight. It was actually quite decent at that point with the exception of being at a temperature warmer than optimal to drink. My opinion of this wine still hasn’t changed, in that I’m not interested in jumping through hoops to make it palatable. Would 5 years in the bottle result in something worthwhile? I’m really not interested in finding out.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: Slightly confused as to the oak “blowing off” comment (oak doesn’t tend to blow off, in my experience), but I think Olivier Leflaive’s wines are OK. For a while, given what was going on at Domaine Leflaive, I think he was probably making better wines. Not my personal style of white Burgundy, but I also think you could do a lot worse.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
How does 2014 compare to other vintages? better/worse?

A lifetime ago, I used to live in the same village as an importer/distributor for them, and the summer town picnic used to feature 10-20 cases of their lineup being poured with burgers/dogs etc!

posts.name: L. ZHU
posts.cooked:
How does 2014 compare to other vintages? better/worse?

A lifetime ago, I used to live in the same village as an importer/distributor for them, and the summer town picnic used to feature 10-20 cases of their lineup being poured with burgers/dogs etc!

I’ve tried their 2014 Abbaye de Morgeot which is a Domaine bottling. Good quality white with freshness and acidity, but a bit of fat to make it enjoyable and at $70-80 a bottle, it was priced fairly well

Per William’s comment, I think some of the Domaine wines are worth a look

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
PXL_20231201_015023021~21920×2439 234 KB

I too was underwhelmed by this bottling, in this case the 2014 Olivier Leflaive [Chassagne Montrachet]. 13% on the label, medium bodied, lemon on the palate, and a sweet vanilla oaky nose. It was enjoyable, but a poor value on my scale. I like whites on the young/fresh side anyways, so have actually preferred this negociant’s lower level bottlings (e.g. Les Setilles) near release. Sealed with DIAM10, which was a nice touch. Overall, it gets a B, and I would not repurchase.

PXL_20231201_025508259~21920×1122 290 KB

Supper was a slab of salmon with crispy mushrooms, and pan charred Brussels. I liked all that more than the wine.
